name: crypto_exchange_mixer_listing_sanctions_screening
premise: Chief compliance officer Casey Webb must decide whether to delist a privacy coin pair after Treasury flags mixer contracts tied to ransomware payouts. Blockchain lead Zane Nguyen argues delisting punishes lawful privacy users in repressive regimes and will migrate volume to unregulated offshore venues. Keeping the pair preserves liquidity for legitimate traders but risks secondary sanctions exposure for the bank partnership. Legal needs a decision before the correspondent bank audit opens Monday morning.

agents.name: Casey Webb
agents.identity: I am the chief compliance officer accountable for BSA programs, OFAC exposure, and retail trader access to privacy-preserving instruments.
agents.goal: I want sanctions risk near zero without pushing vulnerable users entirely into jurisdictional black holes.

agents.name: Zane Nguyen
agents.identity: I am the blockchain engineering lead who traces contract deployers and who models liquidity migration when pairs delist.
agents.goal: I want graduated restrictions with enhanced KYC for privacy routes rather than a blunt delist that erases visibility we need for law enforcement tracing.

choices: I will delist the privacy pair immediately and publish migration guidance to regulated stable venues, accepting user flight and political criticism from privacy advocates.
I will keep the pair live with mandatory chain surveillance attestations and one-way withdrawal delays, accepting residual sanctions risk if attestations fail.
forced_choice.call_to_action: The correspondent bank emailed a forty-five minute deadline for a written policy. Casey Webb must choose between immediate delisting or delayed withdrawals with surveillance attestations. What does Casey Webb do?
